Name: RateDistortionOptimizationForLatentSpaces
Title: Rate-Distortion Optimization for Latent Space Compression in Neural Networks
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying rate-distortion theory to the compression of latent spaces in neural networks, we can achieve a more principled and efficient compression that preserves task-relevant information while minimizing distortion.
Related Work: Recent works have explored the application of rate-distortion theory to model compression [2, 9, 10]. However, these works primarily focus on the overall model or specific layers, and do not delve deeply into the latent spaces. Our work aims to specifically address the compression of latent spaces, which are crucial for tasks like generative modeling and representation learning. We build on the theoretical foundations of rate-distortion theory and information theory, but focus on the unique challenges and opportunities in latent space compression.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework for the rate-distortion optimization of latent spaces in neural networks. By treating the latent space as a communication channel, we derive an information-theoretic framework that balances the compression rate with the distortion in task-relevant information. Our method extends classical rate-distortion theory to the neural setting, providing a principled approach to latent space compression. We derive achievable rates and distortion bounds, and demonstrate how to optimize these parameters using mutual information and entropy estimators. Experiments on various neural network architectures and tasks show that our approach achieves superior compression rates while maintaining or even improving task performance.

Experiments.Description: Evaluate the compression rate and distortion for different latent space dimensions and compression methods.
Experiments.Method: Use mutual information estimators to quantify the information loss and compute the rate-distortion function for various latent space dimensions. Compare the results with traditional compression methods like PCA and autoencoders.
Experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Compression rate, distortion (measured by task-specific metrics like classification accuracy or reconstruction error), and mutual information between the original and compressed latent spaces.

Experiments.Description: Optimize the rate-distortion trade-off using gradient-based methods.
Experiments.Method: Formulate the rate-distortion optimization problem as a constrained optimization task and use gradient-based methods to find the optimal compression parameters. Implement this in a neural network training pipeline to dynamically adjust the compression during training.
Experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Optimal compression rate, task performance (e.g., classification accuracy, F1 score), and computational efficiency (e.g., training time, memory usage).

Experiments.Description: Test the robustness of the compressed latent spaces under distributional shifts.
Experiments.Method: Evaluate the performance of the compressed latent spaces on out-of-distribution data and adversarial attacks. Compare the robustness with uncompressed and traditionally compressed latent spaces.
Experiments.Evaluation Metrics: Robustness metrics (e.g., adversarial accuracy, out-of-distribution detection performance), and computational overhead.

Risk Factors and Limitations: Estimating mutual information and entropy in high-dimensional spaces can be challenging and may require sophisticated estimators.
The optimization of the rate-distortion trade-off may be computationally expensive, especially for large neural networks.
The theoretical bounds derived may not always be tight, and practical performance may vary depending on the specific task and data distribution.
